sourceMovie.title: Rocky
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Rocky (1976) stuck because it commits to its drama premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. John G. Avildsen's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The film leans hard into its core genre, which is exactly what fans say they wanted more of from blockbusters in that era.

recommendations.title: Gladiator
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Rocky clicked for you, Gladiator (2000) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Léon: The Professional
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Rocky clicked for you, Léon: The Professional (1994) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Edward Scissorhands
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Rocky clicked for you, Edward Scissorhands (1990) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama, with emotional whiplash that feels honest. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Rocky?
faq.answer: Try Gladiator, Léon: The Professional, Edward Scissorhands—each shares DNA with Rocky while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Rocky?
faq.answer: Rocky (1976) is best described as Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Rocky part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Rocky Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
